article.para: Tags are used to refer to images. A tag forms a part of the image's name. Unlike labels, tags can be freely defined, and they are usually used to indicate a version number.
If a tag exists in multiple images, the newest image is used. The image maintainer decides which tags to assign to the container image.
The conventional tag format is repository name: image version specification (usually version number). For example, the tag for the latest published image of &slea; 15 SP2 would be suse/sle15:15.2.

important.title: The latest tag is migrating to &suselinux; 16
important.para: The base of the &bcia; family is migrating from &slsa; 15 to &suselinux; 16. The migration is staged image by image, not flipped at once. bci-nano is the first general-purpose image to move, and the core-infrastructure images (bci-base, bci-init, bci-minimal, bci-micro, bci-busybox) will follow later. SUSE will communicate the timing of each flip through the usual channels (the SUSE blog and the SUSE/bci discussions board). Until each flip happens, pulling registry.suse.com/bci/bci-base:latest still gives you a 15 SP7-based image. Check the live registry page or cat /etc/os-release in the image to confirm the current target of :latest for any given image.

See
and
for the original announcement and the up-to-date status of the per-image flips.
